---
title: "What is Delta Method?"
description: |
  A procedure that uses the Taylor series expansion of a function of one or more random variables to obtain approximations to the expected value of the function and to its variance.
author:
  - name: Longxiang Wu
    url: https://lunghsiang-wu.github.io/longxiangwu/
date: 2024-12-10
preview: schematic.png
output:
  distill::distill_article:
    self_contained: false
    includes:
      in_header: header.html
---


```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
```

A procedure that uses the *Taylor series expansion* of a function of one or more
random variables to obtain approximations to the expected value of the function and to its
variance. For example, writing a variable $x$ as $x = \mu + \epsilon$ where $E(x) = \mu$ and $E(\epsilon) = 0$,
Taylor's expansion gives

$$f(x) = f(\mu) + \epsilon \frac{df(x)}{dx}\bigg|_{x=\mu} + \frac{\epsilon^2}{2} \frac{d^2f(x)}{dx^2}\bigg|_{x=\mu} + \cdots$$

If terms involving $\epsilon^2, \epsilon^3$, etc. are assumed to be negligible then

$$f(x) \approx f(\mu) + (x - \mu)f'(\mu)$$

So that

$$\text{var}[f(x)] \approx [f'(\mu)]^2 \text{var}(x)$$

So if $f(x) = \ln x$ then $\text{var}(\ln x) = (1/\mu^2)\text{var}(x)$.
